e-what-screen-kept.content:
### What Screen Design Kept

Screen design inherited the typographic paragraph, the column, the headline hierarchy, the caption, the pull-quote, and the reading rhythm of horizontal left-to-right text. It kept the concept of the page — even when the page became infinite scroll.

It kept the masthead logic of print — logo, navigation, primary content — repurposed as a header bar. It kept the index logic of the book — contents, chapters, sections — repurposed as navigation menus and site maps.

Understanding where these conventions came from makes it easier to question whether they belong in contexts where print's original constraints no longer apply.

e-what-screen-lost.content:
### What Screen Design Lost

The physical object. Print's conventions were shaped by the fact that a book or newspaper occupies space, has weight, opens in a specific direction, and is held in a specific way. These physical facts produced legibility conventions — margin widths, leading ratios, binding allowances — that screen design adopted without the physical constraints that motivated them.

The result is a body of typographic convention that is partially applicable and partially cargo. The designer's task is to know which is which: which conventions earn their place in screen contexts, and which are habits waiting to be reconsidered.

e-reading.content:
### Further Reading

Bringhurst, R. — *The Elements of Typographic Style* (1992)

Tschichold, J. — *The New Typography* (1928, trans. 1995)

Meggs, P.B. — *A History of Graphic Design* (1983)

Lupton, E. — *Thinking with Type* (2004)

Hollis, R. — *Graphic Design: A Concise History* (1994)

McLuhan, M. — *The Gutenberg Galaxy* (1962)
